replies.text: I just couldn't really get into S4 Witcher. Liam could've at least done the voice. I may have been more forgiving, but he didnt even do the voice. And he was more.... emotional than Geralt has ever been. I felt more like I was watching a Hemsworth Thor season than a Witcher season. When Henry Geralt showed true emotion, it wasnt sappy and tacky, it had meaning amd solidarity. It made impact. It meant something. When Liam Geralt does it, its too often and entirely too mushy, expected by episode 3 so much that it doesnt surprise you, its predictable, and lost its meaningful impact. Aka, its often, and entirely too mushy for Geralt. Yennefer is more Henry Geralt/true Geralt, than Liam Geralt is. For that matter, so is Ciri this season. It was weird and predictable this season, and I hated that. I think i speak for all Gemini here, we get bored easily if its predictable, as our brains tend to literally predict words and emotions and actions whilst watching a TV series/movie. If we get surprised, its therefore memorable and we immediately restart the entire movie/season series 🤣 😂 the previous seasons had so much witt and almost hidden gem humor, surprising emotion or lack thereof, that we easily restart the entire series as soon as we finish so that we can catch things we missed. Liam doesnt bring that to the table. It felt almost like.. PG-13 preteen Thors Witcher, like we were watching a DC/Marvel series or something. And that's boring af. Yasker was the only reason I kept watching it lol he didnt change at all and that's amazing. Even his soft heartfelt side shown brighter ALMOST made up for the Liam Thor Hemsworth disappointment

LMAO, I mean I'm sure you never read the ASOIAF books so I don't mean to be a dooche, but the Game of Thrones showrunners are two of some of the most creatively bankrupt-talentless-arseholes that didn't remotely care about the books / lore or even the creators input. Seriously, some of the worst possible candidates who could've gotten that job, and to this day I'm convinced they only got it because the one's daddy was a big-mug at Goldman Sachs  . . . . They had VIRTUALLY next to no experience in film / TV / writing before Game of Thrones, I kid you not, one wrote some obscure book nobody cared about and one helped write a terrible X-Men movie. That's IT, but somehow found themselves at the helm of a multi-million dollar HBO frontrunner show that was only saved after the disastrous pilot by the likes of Terrance Winters'. 

But not only did they not care about book/lore accuracy or dialogue or the fantasy aspect of ASOIAF, but they clearly know NOTHING about medieval warfare / history and couldn't even be bothered to have historians and advisors on set. 

For crying out loud, in the show they actually had Bronn say "as pretty as a picture" . . A PICTURE. When Samwell goes to be a "maester," not only does he take his 'girlfriend' and "son" with him TO THE CITADEL (and his family's ancestral valaryian steel sword his dad would 100% track him down for), but somehow-someway he gets to live with them in a BANGING "apartment" (that would cost a ton of coin he can't afford) outside of the Citadel (unlike any other training/studying to be a maester). THEN, he spends most of his time cleaning shit buckets and serving food (which they 100% do not do), finds a "cure" for GREYSCALE which he's verbally reprimanded for (and is never mentioned again), tells all the fuddy-duddy maesters about the "white walkers" and magic (which they would've literally murdered him for hence why in the book he doesn't say JACK to anybody but Marwyn The Mage) only to quit out early and somehow wind up "Grandmaester" by the end (which is the equivalent to somebody quitting the military in boot camp and somehow becoming a Joint Chief of Staff 5 years later).

replies.author: @LongJohnathan
replies.text:
​@itsnotycf​ I'll preface by admitting I started watching the show casually when I was maybe 14, (2015), I wasn't a book fan then so for a few years initially I was a "show normie." Then I read an AWOIAF wiki article and thought "whoa what the **? Commander Mormont has a magical pet raven that's always on his shoulder and there's all these other characters up here like Donal Noye and Stonesnake and Eben and Coter Pyke and Brown Ben Plum? Winterfell has a "Winter Town" beneath it, and is a stone castle with a stone ground (dirt den in the show) and a dry moat between two sets of wall surrounding it? Wait, Jaime doesn't go down to the Riverlands with Bronn; but his two new squires (who also fought for House Stark in the war), two horses name "Honor" and "Glory," another boy squire who fought in the Blackwater, a badass Lord named Strongboar Crakehall, his wicked cool cousin Daven Lannister, and Ser Flement Brax with the sickest pony armor drip in the realm? And Edmure doesn't just open the gates immediately and the Blackfish doesn't die there babbling how he "hasn't had a sword fight in years" despite being one of the main instruments in Robb Stark's campaign?" 

I'm sure you remember how one of the major "enticing" aspects to the show was "DUDE there's all kinds of bewbs and s3x!?" Needless-graphic-full on s3x scenes, and I've had plenty of hate-rewatches where I skip them but always see how much time they needlessly take up, and almost every single one is at least 5 minutes long. Some take up to 9-10 minutes, aka almost 1/4th of an entire episode. In contrast, they butchered or even left out nearly every single (often badass and compelling) speech or long bit of monolog any character ever made. King Stannis' "Peach" speech to Davos? Nah ** all that. Jaime Lannister telling Catelyn the true story of how Brandon Stark died? Meh, cut that out, boil the scene down to a few minutes cause we need bewbs. Prince Doran Martell's reveal to his daughter Arienne of the Dornish Master Plan? Nah just girl-boss kinslay him. 

They only dedicated a measly few minutes to the epic parlay with King Stannis and (dainty-feminine) Renly Baratheon, left out about 75% of the amazing dialogue whilst butchering what little was kept in with modern writing/dialect; while making sure we got about 7 minutes full fledged gay p0rn / awkward scene of a blatantly gay fem-boy being uncomfortable around his new wife (not even close to who Renly is in the book BTW, he's literally a smaller-more smug and arrogant-Robert) that also was never a scene in the book (and is something that literally ruins the intrigue and mystery surrounding Margery's "virtue" and "maidenhead").

The "King of the North" scene in the books is maybe 10min of reading, in a scene that could easily be reduced to 5-6 solid minutes (in the show it's maybe 3), one of the most badass and compelling scenes in all of the books. It also involved all of the major Lords of the North and Riverlands and Trident, but in the show you quite literally only know who Robb/Cat and The Greatjon are, with the rest looking like a shabby bunch of country bumpkins (instead of wealthy-powerful Lords in colorful fur lined cloaks and satin and ornate armor). Most of the dialogue that leads to Greatjon declaring Robb their King was completely cut out, not to mention Catelyn's desperate plea to make a peace and "go home," which while ridiculous is still touching and compelling and understandable. It was an integral bit of dialogue as well, because her plea for peace makes them ponder for a moment, but then quickly makes them dig down even harder, "what have mine and yours died for if all is to be put back in place?" Blackwood and Bracken, two ancient houses with an ancient blood fued, even agree with each other for once. 

The way dialogue was written was also another major problem. It was dumbed down and written for "NFL players and soccer moms" (I kid you not that's a real quote about the kind of audience they wanted to make the show for). Nobody truly spoke like it's the medieval era / Westeros outside of season one (and even then . .). "On the morrow, mummers, name (birth) day, break my fast (breakfast), the others (white walkers) take you, a moons turn (month), lickspittles." People say "fart" and "picture" and "tomorrow" and "birthday" and "paper" (not "parchment" or "scroll"). Especially midway-later on my God, it all culminated in a painful reminder I'm watching Hollywood actors larp. 

Short end of it is D&D didn't give a flying ** about the source material, they wanted 7 seasons initially with that 7th consisting of 6 measly episodes. They were doing one book PER season . . . The way the show was crafted was made to be "bingeable" for casual audiences; 2-4min scenes going all over the damn place in no sequential order, one minute in Kings Landing the next the wall then to Qarth then Winterfell then Kings Landing again. The show is basically a giant loose cable knit sweater, you pull one single thread you can unravel this entire son of a ** 😂 Granted I'm a 'book snob' and I can also concede EVERYTHING can't be shown, I get it, but they didn't even show half of what could've been.

author: @captbuckyohare
text:
Witcher S1: Reasonably faithful to the books/short stories resulting in a "this has potential" reception.

Witcher S2: Spends 2 1/2 episodes actually adapting content from Blood of Elves (badly). Invents entirely new stories for Yennefer and Ciri. Deletes Triss' story and two Geralt plotlines (one with Ciri, one as a sort of manhunt, western man on a mission story), and completely fumbles the Yen/Ciri relationship and assassinates Yen's character resulting in a "this has gone way off the rails" reception

Witcher S3: Realises S2's choices for Yen and Ciri were disastrous. Attempts to handwave and move on, loosely adapting elements of BOF, Time of Contempt, and Blood of Elves. Characters are aged up and turned gay so they can bonk. Key events from the main story are left out entirely because now they don't make sense in the context of S2's fallout. The plotlines they do attempt to adapt are handled clumsily. The show never recovers from S2 and Cavill's impending exit results in a "this is only going to get worse isn't it?" reception.

Witcher S4: Panics and attempts to broadly adapt the remainder of Baptism of Fire, a novel that mainly focusses on Geralt's road to recovery and his journey towards choosing a side in a war over and above his desire to protect Ciri, all done through his interactions with Regis, Milva, Zoltan, and Cahir. The show can't do this because it has butchered so many of the players and insists on de-emphasising Geralt's story that it all feels flat and C-Plot-ish. The Rats story is adapted, but completely bastardised - along with what was potentially on the table for Ciri's character.  In the books, The Rats are a murderous band of killers and thieves and draw Ciri into a dark world of murder and survival. She becomes deeply depressed and develops a lust for killing. In the books, she is presented as bisexual and has a a relationship with the Rat's leader - but it's presented as a means to survival and a toxic co-dependent arrangement. The Rat's dark reputation catches up with them when... well the show gets that bit right. But they're not good people. They're not Robin Hood. It's pretty funny how much the show self-consciously felt the need to "fix them". Yen is again handed a plotline that belonged to someone else in the books - Phillipa is the one who sets out to build the lodge, not Yen. Yen is actually against it, instead focussing on finding Ciri along with her own schemes - so once again, the butchers Yen and gives her someone else's storyline. She stops looking for Ciri to spend a third of the show building her army and finding Vilgafortz. Her role and plotline are massively inflated in the show (again). This has resulted in a "wow it really did get worse didn't it?" reception.

Witcher S5: Profit?

replies.author: @thatdamhorserescue
replies.text: I just couldn't really get into S4 Witcher. Liam could've at least done the voice. I may have been more forgiving, but he didnt even do the voice. And he was more.... emotional than Geralt has ever been. I felt more like I was watching a Hemsworth Thor season than a Witcher season. When Henry Geralt showed true emotion, it wasnt sappy and tacky, it had meaning amd solidarity. It made impact. It meant something. When Liam Geralt does it, its too often and entirely too mushy, expected by episode 3 so much that it doesnt surprise you, its predictable, and lost its meaningful impact. Aka, its often, and entirely too mushy for Geralt. Yennefer is more Henry Geralt/true Geralt, than Liam Geralt is. For that matter, so is Ciri this season. It was weird and predictable this season, and I hated that. I think i speak for all Gemini here, we get bored easily if its predictable, as our brains tend to literally predict words and emotions and actions whilst watching a TV series/movie. If we get surprised, its therefore memorable and we immediately restart the entire movie/season series 🤣 😂 the previous seasons had so much witt and almost hidden gem humor, surprising emotion or lack thereof, that we easily restart the entire series as soon as we finish so that we can catch things we missed. Liam doesnt bring that to the table. It felt almost like.. PG-13 preteen Thors Witcher, like we were watching a DC/Marvel series or something. And that's boring af. Yasker was the only reason I kept watching it lol he didnt change at all and that's amazing. Even his soft heartfelt side shown brighter ALMOST made up for the Liam Thor Hemsworth disappointment

replies.text:
@thatdamhorserescue It's all very obvious during the "burn the witch" confrontation. When Hemsworth confronts the priest and argues for the woman and girl's freedom, it comes across as flat and wet. When the girl is cut down by the Nilfguard rider Hemsworth's reaction feels.... functional. I picture Cavil (at his peak - he stopped caring during S3), and imagine, even if it would still have been a narrative mess, how much more impactful his performance and presence and emotional reactions would have been.

It's night and day.
